name: best-practices
context: Dockerfile authoring, image optimization, security hardening, layer caching, and production container best practices
info: This guide consolidates Docker best practices across the entire ecosystem: writing efficient Dockerfiles, minimizing image size, securing containers, optimizing layer caching, managing secrets, configuring health checks, setting resource limits, and structuring production deployments. Best practices are organized by category -- Dockerfile authoring, image optimization, security hardening, runtime configuration, logging, and CI/CD integration. Following these practices produces smaller, faster, more secure containers. This guide references specific skills for implementation details: [[docker/images/build]] for Dockerfile authoring, [[docker/images/multi-stage]] for size optimization, [[docker/containers/run]] for runtime security, and [[docker/containers/logs]] for logging configuration.
body:
# Docker Best Practices

This guide consolidates best practices for building, securing, and running Docker containers in production. Following these practices produces smaller images, faster builds, and more secure deployments.

## Dockerfile Authoring

### Use Minimal Base Images

Start from the smallest base that meets your requirements:

| Base Image | Size | Shell | Package Manager | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `scratch` | 0 MB | No | No | Go/Rust static binaries |
| `distroless` | 2-20 MB | No | No | Java, Python, Node.js (runtime only) |
| `alpine` | 5 MB | Yes | apk | General purpose, smaller than Debian |
| `*-slim` | 50-80 MB | Yes | apt | Debian-based, smaller than full |
| `ubuntu/debian` | 100-200 MB | Yes | apt | Full OS, maximum compatibility |

```dockerfile
# Good: minimal base
FROM node:20-alpine

# Avoid: full base with unnecessary packages
# FROM node:20
```

### Pin Image Versions

Never use `latest` or bare tags in production:

```dockerfile
# Good: pinned version
FROM node:20.11.1-alpine3.19

# Acceptable: minor version pin
FROM node:20-alpine

# Bad: mutable tags
# FROM node:latest
# FROM node
```

For maximum reproducibility, pin by digest:
```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine@sha256:abc123...
```

### Optimize Layer Caching

Order instructions from least to most frequently changed:

```dockerfile
# 1. Base image (changes rarely)
FROM node:20-alpine

# 2. System dependencies (changes occasionally)
RUN apk add --no-cache curl

# 3. Application dependencies (changes when deps update)
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci --production

# 4. Application source (changes frequently)
COPY . .

# 5. Build step
RUN npm run build
```

See [[docker/images/build#caching]] for advanced caching strategies.

### Combine RUN Commands

Each RUN creates a layer. Combine related commands and clean up in the same layer:

```dockerfile
# Good: single layer, clean up in same step
RUN apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends curl ca-certificates && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Bad: multiple layers, apt cache persists
# RUN apt-get update
# RUN apt-get install -y curl
```

### Use .dockerignore

Exclude files that should not enter the build context:

```
node_modules
.git
.gitignore
*.md
README*
dist
build
coverage
.env*
.DS_Store
Dockerfile*
docker-compose*
.dockerignore
.github
.vscode
```

A proper `.dockerignore` speeds up builds and prevents cache invalidation from irrelevant file changes.

### Use Multi-Stage Builds

Separate build-time and runtime dependencies. See [[docker/images/multi-stage]] for detailed patterns.

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=builder /app/package.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
CMD ["node", "dist/server.js"]
```

## Security Hardening

### Run as Non-Root

Never run containers as root in production:

```dockerfile
# Create a non-root user
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup

# ... install dependencies as root ...

# Switch to non-root before CMD
USER appuser
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
```

At runtime, enforce with `--user`:
```bash
docker run --user 1000:1000 myapp
```

### Never Store Secrets in Images

Secrets in build args, COPY, or ENV are visible in image history:

```dockerfile
# Bad: secret in build arg (visible in docker history)
# ARG DB_PASSWORD
# ENV DB_PASSWORD=$DB_PASSWORD

# Good: BuildKit secret mount (not stored in any layer)
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=db_password \
    DB_PASSWORD=$(cat /run/secrets/db_password) && \
    npm run migrate
```

At runtime, use Docker secrets or mount from external secret managers. See [[docker/containers/run#security]].

### Read-Only Filesystem

```bash
# Run with read-only root filesystem
docker run --read-only --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,size=50m myapp
```

### Drop Capabilities

```bash
# Drop all capabilities, add only what is needed
docker run --cap-drop ALL --cap-add NET_BIND_SERVICE myapp
```

### Scan for Vulnerabilities

```bash
# Docker Scout
docker scout cves myapp:latest

# Trivy
trivy image myapp:latest

# Snyk
snyk container test myapp:latest
```

Integrate scanning into CI/CD to block vulnerable images from being pushed. See [[docker/images#vulnerability-scanning]].

### Prevent Privilege Escalation

```bash
docker run --security-opt no-new-privileges myapp
```

## Image Size Optimization

### Size Reduction Checklist

1. Use multi-stage builds -- see [[docker/images/multi-stage]]
2. Start from minimal base images (alpine, slim, distroless, scratch)
3. Remove package manager caches in the same RUN layer
4. Use `--no-install-recommends` with apt-get
5. Use `--no-cache` with apk add
6. Exclude dev dependencies: `npm ci --omit=dev`, `pip install --no-dev`
7. Remove build artifacts, docs, and test files in the final stage
8. Use `.dockerignore` to keep the build context small

### Checking Image Size

```bash
# List images sorted by size
docker images --format '{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}} {{.Size}}' | sort -k2 -h

# Analyze layers
docker history myapp:latest

# Deep analysis with dive (third-party)
dive myapp:latest
```

## Runtime Configuration

### Health Checks

Always define health checks for production containers:

```dockerfile
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=10s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
```

See [[docker/compose#healthchecks]] for health checks in Compose.

### Resource Limits

Always set resource limits to prevent containers from consuming all host resources:

```bash
docker run -d \
  --memory 512m \
  --memory-reservation 256m \
  --cpus 1.0 \
  --pids-limit 100 \
  myapp
```

See [[docker/containers#resource-limits]] for details.

### Restart Policies

```bash
# Production: restart unless manually stopped
docker run -d --restart unless-stopped myapp

# CI/testing: never restart
docker run -d --restart no myapp
```

### Logging

Configure log rotation to prevent disk exhaustion:

```bash
docker run -d \
  --log-opt max-size=10m \
  --log-opt max-file=3 \
  myapp
```

Write logs to stdout/stderr in structured JSON format. See [[docker/containers/logs]] for logging drivers and centralized logging.

## CI/CD Practices

### Build Cache in CI

```bash
# Export cache to registry
docker buildx build \
  --cache-from type=registry,ref=myapp:cache \
  --cache-to type=registry,ref=myapp:cache,mode=max \
  -t myapp:$CI_COMMIT_SHA .
```

See [[docker/images/build#buildkit]] for cache export/import.

### Tag Strategy

Tag every image with:
1. Git commit SHA for traceability
2. Semantic version for releases
3. `latest` only on the default branch

```bash
docker tag myapp:latest myapp:$GIT_SHA
docker tag myapp:latest myapp:v1.2.3
```

See [[docker/images#tagging-strategy]] for details.

### Production Deployment Checklist

- [ ] Multi-stage build with minimal runtime image
- [ ] Specific version tags (no `latest` in manifests)
- [ ] Non-root user in Dockerfile
- [ ] HEALTHCHECK defined
- [ ] Resource limits configured
- [ ] Log rotation configured
- [ ] Secrets managed externally (not baked into image)
- [ ] Vulnerability scan passing
- [ ] .dockerignore excludes unnecessary files
- [ ] Read-only filesystem where possible
- [ ] Capabilities dropped to minimum needed
- [ ] Restart policy set

sections.title: Dockerfile authoring
sections.body: Use minimal base images (alpine, slim, distroless, scratch). Pin image versions for reproducibility. Order instructions from least to most frequently changed for optimal caching. Combine RUN commands to reduce layers and clean up in the same step. Use .dockerignore to exclude irrelevant files from the build context. Use multi-stage builds to separate build and runtime. See [[docker/images/build]] and [[docker/images/multi-stage]].

sections.title: Security hardening
sections.body: Run as non-root (USER instruction or --user flag). Never store secrets in images -- use BuildKit secret mounts or external secret managers. Use read-only filesystems with tmpfs for writable paths. Drop all capabilities and add only needed ones. Scan images for CVEs with Docker Scout, Trivy, or Snyk. Prevent privilege escalation with --security-opt no-new-privileges.

sections.title: Image size optimization
sections.body: Multi-stage builds reduce size by 50-99%. Use minimal base images. Remove caches in the same RUN layer. Exclude dev dependencies. Analyze with docker history or dive. Track image size in CI to catch regressions. Smaller images mean faster pulls, less storage, and reduced attack surface.

sections.title: Runtime configuration
sections.body: Define HEALTHCHECK for monitoring. Set memory and CPU limits to prevent resource exhaustion. Configure restart policies for resilience. Enable log rotation to prevent disk fill. Use --read-only and --cap-drop for defense in depth. Set --pids-limit to prevent fork bombs. See [[docker/containers]] for details.

sections.title: CI/CD practices
sections.body: Export build cache to registries for faster CI builds. Tag images with git commit SHA for traceability and semantic versions for releases. Scan images before pushing. Use build matrix for multi-platform images. Automate the entire build-scan-push-deploy pipeline. Never push unscanned images to production registries.
